findings.description: Services (Auth, Job, Application) publish events to RabbitMQ directly within @Service methods. Fallbacks only log errors or save locally without guaranteed eventual consistency. If the message broker is unavailable during a database commit, events are lost.
findings.recommendation: Implement the Transactional Outbox pattern. Save events to an 'outbox' table in the same transaction as the business data, and use a background poller to publish to RabbitMQ.

findings.description: Events are constructed as untyped Maps (Map<String, Object>) with hardcoded routing keys ('job.created', 'user.registered'). This prevents schema evolution and increases the risk of downstream consumer breakage during upgrades.
findings.recommendation: Adopt a Centralized Schema Registry (e.g., Spring Cloud Stream Schema Registry) and use versioned POJOs for all events.

findings.description: RabbitMQ configurations in application.yml lack 'dead-letter-exchange' or 'auto-bind-dlq' settings. Failed message processing will either be lost (if not requeued) or cause 'poison pill' retry loops that exhaust resources.
findings.recommendation: Configure standard DLQ patterns for all RabbitMQ listeners with exponential backoff retries.

findings.description: The 'Course' entity embeds a List of 'Lesson' objects directly. If courses grow large, this risk hitting the 16MB document limit and causes significant I/O overhead for partial updates.
findings.recommendation: Migrate large child collections to separate documents with ID references or use MongoDB '$push' with slice limits.

findings.description: SharedSecurityConfig allows all origins ('*'). While the API Gateway should handle strict CORS, the individual services should still have a defense-in-depth configuration.
findings.recommendation: Restrict allowed origins to the specific API Gateway and Frontend internal URLs.
